title: Śatadūṣaṇī — Vedānta Deśika's hundred refutations of Advaita
attacker_text: Śatadūṣaṇī (c. 1300); supplemented by Nyāya-Pariśuddhi and Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa
target_position_summary: The fully-developed post-Śaṅkara Advaita establishment as Vedānta Deśika engages it: Maṇḍana's Brahma-Siddhi, Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi, Vāchaspati's Bhāmatī, Padmapāda's Pañcapādikā, Prakāśātman's Vivaraṇa, Sarvajñātman's Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka, and Śrīharṣa's Khaṇḍana-Khaṇḍa-Khādya. The Śatadūṣaṇī comprises one hundred numbered dūṣaṇas (refutations) operating argument-by-argument rather than thematically, extending Rāmānuja's saptavidhānupapatti into a more granular and more technically equipped polemic.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-1: against nirviśeṣa-Brahman as cognitive content
premises: Cognition is always cognition-of-something-determinate; the very structure of cognition is intentional in the analytic sense (it has a content, distinguishable from its act).
Cognition of Brahman would either have determinate content (in which case Brahman is not nirviśeṣa) or no determinate content (in which case it is not cognition).
Even the supposed akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti must, qua vṛtti, have some character that distinguishes it from other vṛttis; this character is itself a viśeṣa.
Therefore the Advaitin's central concept (cognitive realization of nirviśeṣa-Brahman) is structurally incoherent.
arguments.inference: Nirviśeṣa-Brahman cannot be the content of any cognition.
arguments.conclusion: The very target of the Advaita soteriology is unattainable on Advaita's own analysis of cognition.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Vivaraṇa's akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti doctrine is the principal target.

positions_left_intact.position: Śaṅkara's bhāṣya is more nuanced about nirguṇa: he distinguishes svarūpa-lakṣaṇa (which Brahman has, sat-cit-ānanda) from taṭastha-lakṣaṇa (which Brahman does not have qua nirguṇa). The argument applies to a more strongly negative reading of nirviśeṣa than Śaṅkara himself committed to.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi addresses the cognitive-incoherence charge by distinguishing svarūpa-jñāna (Brahman's intrinsic self-luminosity) from vṛtti-jñāna (cognitive modification); the latter is intentional and viśiṣṭa, the former is not, and liberation involves the vṛtti dissolving into the svarūpa
later_responses.verdict: preserves the doctrine by distinguishing two registers of cognition; the disagreement reduces to whether non-intentional self-luminosity is itself a coherent notion of cognition.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive. The disagreement concerns the nature of cognition itself, not merely the doctrinal vocabulary. Real metaphysical question.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-2 (representative of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-exegesis dūṣaṇas): against the Advaita reading of neti-neti
premises: Advaita reads Bṛhadāraṇyaka 2.3.6's neti neti as denying all attributes of Brahman, establishing nirviśeṣa-Brahman.

But neti neti, in its scriptural context, is a denial-formula applied to enumerated empirically-determining attributes (the foregoing description of Brahman as gross, subtle, etc.); it is not a global attribute-negation operator.
The Sanskrit syntax of the passage supports the limited-negation reading: neti neti is in apposition to the foregoing enumerated descriptions, not to any-and-all possible predicates.
Hence the passage teaches the absence of empirically-limiting attributes, not the absence of all distinction.
Brahman with svarūpa-attributes (sat-cit-ānanda, jñāna, ānanda, bala, etc., positively predicated by other śruti passages) is consistent with this reading.
arguments.inference: Neti neti does not establish nirviśeṣa-Brahman; the Advaita reading is exegetically strained.
arguments.conclusion: The principal scriptural anchor of nirviśeṣa-vāda fails to support the doctrine.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Śaṅkara's reading of neti neti at BSB 3.2.21–22 and Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya.
Sureśvara's Vārttika on the same passage.

positions_left_intact.position: Bhāmatī's reading is partially sympathetic to the limited-negation construal at the empirical level, though it still endorses nirviśeṣa at the pāramārthika level.

later_responses.response_text: Appayya Dīkṣita's Siddhānta-Leśa-Saṅgraha — surveys the multiple intra-Advaita readings of neti neti and defends the global-negation construal by appeal to the broader śruti context
later_responses.verdict: the dispute reduces to which śruti context is the more authoritative anchor for the passage's interpretation.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive at the exegetical level. The disagreement over how to read neti neti is a real interpretive question with metaphysical consequences.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-15 (representative of the anirvacanīya-khyāti dūṣaṇas): against the Advaita theory of error
premises: Advaita explains perceptual error (e.g., the rope-snake) by anirvacanīya-khyāti: the snake-content is anirvacanīya — neither sat (the snake isn't there) nor asat (it was experienced).
Theories of error are explanatory: they account for why error occurs and what its content is.
Anirvacanīya-khyāti accounts for neither: the indeterminable category leaves both the cause and the content of error unspecified.

Yathārtha-khyāti (the position of Madhva and the Viśiṣṭādvaita-modified version) explains error as misidentification of really-present items: in the rope-snake case, what is actually present is the rope (with snake-like features), and the cognitive error is the identification with the snake-class rather than the rope-class. The cause is partial-feature-presentation; the content is real-but-misclassified.
Yathārtha-khyāti is therefore explanatorily superior.
arguments.inference: The Advaitin theory of error is an empty placeholder; the realist alternatives are genuinely explanatory.
arguments.conclusion: The Advaitin's central epistemological move (anirvacanīya as solvent for both error and avidyā) is dialectically empty.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Ānandabodha's elaborate Pramāṇa-Mālā defense of the indeterminable category.

positions_left_intact.position: Bhāmatī's account of error is closer to anyathā-khyāti in some passages; the polemic engages the indeterminable-category line specifically.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi defends anirvacanīya-khyāti by arguing that the rope-snake case requires positive-content explanation that yathārtha-khyāti (which explains error as mis-classification) cannot supply: the snake-content is not in the rope and is not elsewhere either, hence requires the indeterminable category to be located
later_responses.verdict: the reply requires the disagreement to be over what counts as positive-content of an error; both sides have substantive positions and neither dissolves.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive. Theory of error is a real question with substantive metaphysical consequences. The disagreement persists across the post-Vedānta-Deśika tradition.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-31 (representative of the dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi dūṣaṇas): against world-as-cognition-only
premises: The dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda strand within Advaita (especially Prakāśānanda's later articulation but anticipated in Vivaraṇa) holds that the world has no being apart from being-cognised.

But cognition of the world presupposes a world-distinction (this-rather-than-that) that the cognition cannot itself supply: any cognition that allegedly creates its object must already discriminate that object from others.
The supposed self-creating cognition therefore presupposes the very world-distinctions it is meant to generate.
Hence dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi is structurally circular.
arguments.inference: Dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi-vāda cannot account for the differentiation it would need to account for.
arguments.conclusion: The radical idealist strand of Advaita collapses on its own terms.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Vivaraṇa's idealist tendencies; though dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi is most fully Prakāśānanda's.

positions_left_intact.position: Śaṅkara's bhāṣya does not endorse dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi; the position is post-Śaṅkara.
Bhāmatī's sṛṣṭi-dṛṣṭi-vāda is the alternative within Advaita, less vulnerable to this charge.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi distinguishes dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi from sṛṣṭi-dṛṣṭi as two intra-Advaita positions; defends the more moderate sṛṣṭi-dṛṣṭi against Vedānta Deśika's charge
later_responses.verdict: concedes the dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi extreme is vulnerable; defends the moderate alternative.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive against the extreme idealist strand; engages an Advaita position that is genuinely contested intra-school.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-46 (representative of the Khaṇḍana-Khaṇḍa-Khādya dūṣaṇas): against the anti-definitional method
premises: Śrīharṣa's Khaṇḍana-Khaṇḍa-Khādya defends Advaita by arguing that no rival position can give consistent definitions (lakṣaṇas) of its key categories; therefore no rival position is dialectically defensible.

But Śrīharṣa's own position presupposes definitions: the Khaṇḍana itself uses the categories of pramāṇa, prameya, vikalpa, etc., and treats them as having determinate enough content to permit dialectical exchange.
Any dialectical exchange — including Śrīharṣa's own — presupposes a padārtha-scheme with at least minimal definitional commitments.
Therefore the anti-definitional method is self-undermining: it cannot be conducted without violating its own meta-principle.
arguments.inference: Śrīharṣa's anti-definitional method is performatively self-defeating.
arguments.conclusion: The Advaitin cannot consistently appeal to Śrīharṣa's apparatus to defend Advaita against the constructive realist alternatives.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Citsukha's extension of Śrīharṣa's Khaṇḍana method in Tattva-Pradīpikā.

positions_left_intact.position: Śaṅkara does not employ the Khaṇḍana method; the polemic engages a specific Advaita-defensive resource, not the bhāṣya tradition.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi defends the Khaṇḍana method by distinguishing prātītika-padārthas (those used pragmatically in argument) from pāramārthika-padārthas (those committed to ontologically); the Khaṇḍana operates with the former without affirming the latter
later_responses.verdict: the level-distinction reply preserves the method but at the cost of a level-distinction Vedānta Deśika does not accept; the disagreement reduces to whether the prātītika-pāramārthika distinction is principled.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive on a real methodological question. The disagreement is over the legitimacy of pragmatic-without-committal argumentation, which is itself a substantive position in the philosophy of dialectic.

arguments.name: dūṣaṇa-67 (representative of the kaivalya/mokṣa dūṣaṇas): against identity-as-mokṣa
premises: Advaita defines mokṣa as the cessation of the apparent jīva-Brahman distinction (identity-without-distinction).
But the recognition of identity (aham brahmāsmi) is itself a cognitive act that requires a cognising agent.
If the cognising agent is the jīva, then jīva-distinction persists in the very act of liberation.
If the cognising agent is Brahman, then Brahman undergoes a cognitive change (acquires the recognition), which contradicts Brahman's nirviśeṣa-status.
Either horn defeats the Advaita account of mokṣa.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Śaṅkara's account of mokṣa as cessation of adhyāsa.
Sureśvara's account of mokṣa as akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti.

positions_left_intact.position: Madhva's eternal-service-with-irreducible-distinction is not engaged adversarially; this dūṣaṇa is largely aligned with the Madhva position on mokṣa.

later_responses.response_text: Advaita-Siddhi argues that the recognition is structurally the cessation, not a separate cognitive act; the cessation is identical with what is recognized, and the cognising agent is the locus of the recognition that is itself the dissolution of the cognising agent's distinct status
later_responses.verdict: preserves the doctrine by treating recognition and cessation as the same event; the disagreement reduces to whether such an identity is intelligible.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive on the structure of liberation. Real disagreement; not register-effect.

user_thesis_summary: The Śatadūṣaṇī is the most granular Vedāntic polemic against Advaita extant. Of the hundred dūṣaṇas, the six engaged here are representative of the major thematic clusters (cognitive-content; scripture-exegesis; theory of error; idealist strand; anti-definitional method; mokṣa-structure). On balance, the dūṣaṇas are substantive but with two important qualifications: (i) they target the post-Śaṅkara establishment (Vivaraṇa, Bhāmatī, Citsukha, Śrīharṣa, the Pramāṇa-Mālā) much more than Śaṅkara's bhāṣya, and several do not engage Śaṅkara at all; (ii) Madhusūdana's responses are doctrinally available in nearly every case, and the residual disagreements (often about level-distinctions, dispositional properties, or anirvacanīya predication) are themselves substantive. Vedānta Deśika is more dialectically constructive than Madhva: where Madhva's polemic operates principally by trilemma and reductio, Deśika's operates by detailed analysis of each Advaita move with a corresponding Viśiṣṭādvaita alternative. This makes Deśika's polemic less rhetorically pressuring but more philosophically engaging. Verdict: substantive throughout, with the strongest cases against the Vivaraṇa idealist strand and the weakest cases against Śaṅkara's bhāṣya proper. The polemic does not dissolve under register-clarification; many of the dūṣaṇas are precisely register-clarifications that reveal genuine substantive disagreement underneath surface-similarity.
primary_source_loci: Nyāya-Pariśuddhi — the systematic Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology providing the constructive alternative
Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa — Vedānta Deśika's verse-summary of the school's positive metaphysics
Sarvārtha-Siddhi — Vedānta Deśika's auto-commentary on the Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa

scholarly_apparatus.work: Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita: A Study Based on Vedānta Deśika's Śatadūṣaṇī (Munshiram, 1976)
scholarly_apparatus.position: The standard scholarly reconstruction of the hundred dūṣaṇas; documents the dialectical structure and the post-Śaṅkara targets.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Contribution of Yāmuna to Viśiṣṭādvaita (Madras, 1971), and supplementary works on Vedānta Deśika
scholarly_apparatus.position: Reads Vedānta Deśika as completing the Yāmuna-Rāmānuja polemical project at full Navya-Nyāya rigor; the Śatadūṣaṇī is the technical culmination of saptavidhānupapatti.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Mīmāṃsā / Vedānta in late medieval India (recent scholarly work)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Documents the post-1300 transformation of Vedāntic polemic into formal Navya-Nyāya idiom; Deśika is one of the central figures in this transformation alongside Vyāsatīrtha.
